coredata.dc:title: Carbon and Metal Quantum Dots toxicity on the microalgae Chlorella pyrenoidosa
coredata.dc:creator: Xiao, An
Wang, Chao
Chen, Jiao
Guo, Ruixin
Yan, Zhengyu
Chen, Jianqiu
coredata.dc:description: In this report, we investigated the cytotoxicity of two types of quantum dots(QDs) (carbon quantum dots(CQDs): N, S doped CQDs, N doped CQDs, no doped CQDs; metal QDs(MQDs): CdTe QDs, CdS QDs, CuInS2/ZnS QDs) on Chlorella pyrenoidosa(C. Pyrenoidosa) at different concentrations. We compared the toxicity of different QDs on C. Pyrenoidosa through determination of the algal growth inhibition, acute toxicity tests (EC50), Chlorophyll a(Chla) contents, protein contents, the activity of enzymatic and metabolites contents. When C. Pyrenoidosa was treated by various concentrations of QDs, the Chla contents were consistent to the number of algae cells, showing a good dose-response relationship. At the 96h, the EC50 of N, S doped CQDs, N doped CQDs, no doped CQDs and CdTe QDs, CdS QDs, CuInS2/ZnS QDs were 38.56, 185.83, 232.47, 0.015, 4.88, 459.5mg/l, respectively. The toxicity order of them was: CuInS2/ZnS QDs<no doped CQDs<N doped CQDs<N,S doped CQDs<CdS QDs<CdTe QDs. The activity of antioxidant enzymatic superoxide dismutase (SOD) was enhanced and the non-enzymatic antioxidant glutathion (GSH) level was decreased with the increasing of QDs concentration, respectively. The accumulation of Malondialdehyde (MDA), a product of lipid peroxidation caused by reactive oxygen species(ROS), was enhanced when algae were exposed to QDs. In conclusion, the toxicity of CQDs was smaller than MQDs, but the toxicity of CuInS2/ZnS QDs was the smallest.
coredata.dcterms:subject: QDs
C. Pyrenoidosa
Chla
Protein contents
Enzymatic and metabolites
Toxicity evaluation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effects of six kinds QDs on algal growth
EC50 values for QDs of six kinds
Effects of QDs on Chla contents of C. Pyrenoidosa
Effects of QDs on protein contents of C. Pyrenoidosa
Effects of QDs on enzymatic activities and metabolites of C. Pyrenoidosa

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National College Students Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by fund of College Students Innovation Project for the R&D of Novel Drugs (no. J1030830 ) and the National College Students’ Innovation Entrepreneurial Training Program ( G15019 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effects of CQDs and MQDs on algal growth. (a. N,S doped CQDs, b. N doped CQDs, c. no doped CQDs; d. CdTe QDs, e. CdS QDs, f. CuInS2/ZnS QDs. Values represent the mean value±standard deviation of 3 replicates. Asterisk means significant difference detected at p<0.05 level).
The EC50 values (mg/l) of CQDs and MQDs. The EC50 values (mg/l) of CQDs, b. The EC50 values (mg/l) of MQDs).

Effects of CQDs and MQDs on Chla contents of C. Pyrenoidosa. (a. N, S doped CQDs, b. N doped CQDs, c. no doped CQDs; d. CdTe QDs, e. CdS QDs, f. CuInS2/ZnS QDs. Values represent the mean value±standard deviation of 3 replicates. Asterisk means significant difference detected at p<0.05 vs. control under 144h).

Effects of QDs on protein contents of C. Pyrenoidosa. (a. N, S doped CQDs, b. N doped CQDs, c. no doped CQDs; d. CdTe QDs, e.CdS QDs, f. CuInS2/ZnS QDs. Values represent the mean value±standard deviation of 3 replicates. Asterisk means significant difference detected at p<0.05 level vs. control).
The effects of CQDs and MQDs on the algal growth rate.

row.entry: The exposure conc (mg/l) and average growth rate (AGR) of three kinds of CQDs
The exposure conc (mg/l) and average growth rate (AGR) of three kinds of MQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The effect of CQDs on the algae enzymatic activities and metabolites.
The effect of metal QDs on the algae enzymatic activities and metabolites.

head.ce:title: Carbon and Metal Quantum Dots toxicity on the microalgae Chlorella pyrenoidosa

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 210009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Science, China Pharmaceutical University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing 211000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Science, Nanjing University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Science and Technology, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 210009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life Science and Technology, China Pharmaceutical University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this report, we investigated the cytotoxicity of two types of quantum dots(QDs) (carbon quantum dots(CQDs): N, S doped CQDs, N doped CQDs, no doped CQDs; metal QDs(MQDs): CdTe QDs, CdS QDs, CuInS2/ZnS QDs) on Chlorella pyrenoidosa(C. Pyrenoidosa) at different concentrations. We compared the toxicity of different QDs on C. Pyrenoidosa through determination of the algal growth inhibition, acute toxicity tests (EC50), Chlorophyll a(Chla) contents, protein contents, the activity of enzymatic and metabolites contents. When C. Pyrenoidosa was treated by various concentrations of QDs, the Chla contents were consistent to the number of algae cells, showing a good dose-response relationship. At the 96h, the EC50 of N, S doped CQDs, N doped CQDs, no doped CQDs and CdTe QDs, CdS QDs, CuInS2/ZnS QDs were 38.56, 185.83, 232.47, 0.015, 4.88, 459.5mg/l, respectively. The toxicity order of them was: CuInS2/ZnS QDs<no doped CQDs<N doped CQDs<N,S doped CQDs<CdS QDs<CdTe QDs. The activity of antioxidant enzymatic superoxide dismutase (SOD) was enhanced and the non-enzymatic antioxidant glutathion (GSH) level was decreased with the increasing of QDs concentration, respectively. The accumulation of Malondialdehyde (MDA), a product of lipid peroxidation caused by reactive oxygen species(ROS), was enhanced when algae were exposed to QDs. In conclusion, the toxicity of CQDs was smaller than MQDs, but the toxicity of CuInS2/ZnS QDs was the smallest.

ce:section.ce:para: As the fluorescent nanomaterial, wide varieties of QDs with a variety of unique characteristics are manufactured and used widely (Rosenthal, 2001; Zhang et al., 2013b). MQDs and CQDs are the two types of QDs. Several kinds of MQDs have been employed for biomedical applications as well as manufactured products, including image sensors, emitting materials in LEDs and solar cells (Winnik and Maysinger, 2012). CQDs have excellent prospects in cell markers, biological imaging and other fields (Ray et al., 2009; Chang et al., 2008).

Due to their growing industrial production, a large quantities of nanomaterials can release to the environment and the chance for them to end up in the environment is higher and when them went into the aquatic ecosystems their effects on biological communities are poorly understood (Hardman, 2006; Liu et al., 2009; Wang et al., 2013). Thus, there is growing number of literature regarding the effects of QDs on aquatic organisms (Gagné et al., 2008; Zhao et al., 2009). It was reported that QDs can partially degrade in aqueous environments and release free Cd, and cause greater and specific toxicity than equivalent concentrations of dissolved Cd (Domingos et al., 2011; Ambrosone et al., 2012; Morelli et al., 2012). So far, a few studies have investigated the impacts of QDs on freshwater microalgae (Lin et al., 2009; Bouldin et al., 2008; Domingos et al., 2011; Zhang et al., 2013a) and marine species (Xu et al., 2010).

As primary producers, phytoplankton serves as an important component of the aquatic ecosystem. Microalgae is sensitive to pollutants, and potential environmental impacts could decrease its primary productivity, which could influence the entire food chain and change the structure and function of the whole ecosystem (Jonsson and Aoyama, 2007). Microalgae is easy to culture and propagate, and widely prevalent in lakes and seas (Xiao et al., 2012). So microalgae are excellent aquatic models. Recently, Zhou (Zhou et al., 2016) have investigated the toxicological effect of CdSe/ZnS QDs on four microalgae and analyzed different biological parameters in phyto and zooplanktonic organisms to individuate sensitive endpoints which could be suitable to describe the impact of QDs on marine environment.

The aim of the present paper was to assess toxicological effects of different QDs on planktonic organisms. According to the biological parameters we could compare the toxicity effect of different QDs. Here we have evaluated the toxicity effects of six kinds of QDs (CQDs: N,S doped CQDs, N doped CQDs, no doped CQDs; MQDs: CdTe QDs, CdS QDs, CuInS2/ZnS QDs) using C. Pyrenoidosa as a model organism. Through determination of the algal growth inhibition, acute toxicity tests, Chla contents, protein contents, the activity of enzymatic and metabolites contents we can describe the impacts of QDs on C. Pyrenoidosa. It is the first time to report the toxicity evaluation of the six kinds of QDs synthesized by our laboratory on freshwater algae at the same time and compare the toxicity of different QDs using the same parameters.

C. pyrenoidosa was obtained from the Institute of Hydrobiology (Chinese Academy of Sciences, Wuhan, China). C. Pyrenoidosa was cultivated in 150ml of autoclaved BG-11 medium in 250ml conical flasks under sterile conditions. The BG-11 medium included the following chemical ingredients (mg/l): NaNO3, 1500; K2HPO4, 40; MgSO4·7H2O, 75; CaCl2·2H2O, 36; Na2CO3, 20; EDTANa2, 1; Ferric ammonium citrate, 6; Citric acid, 6; H3BO3, 2.86; MnCl2·4H2O, 1.81; ZnSO4·7H2O, 0.222; NaMoO4·2H2O, 0.39; CuSO4·5H2O, 0.079; Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 0.0494. C. pyrenoidosa was inoculated into BG-11 medium periodically to keep the cells in the logarithmic growth phase and to prepare them for subsequent bioassays. All of the conical flasks were autoclaved in 121°C maintain 30min. The cultivation conditions were as follows: light intensity =4000lx, light/dark ratio=12h: 12h, temperature=(25±1)°C and artificial intermittent shaking (two times per day to ensure optimal growth). The water used in the experiment is ultra pure water.

The QDs used in the test were synthesized by our laboratory method. The detailed synthesis procedures of N,S doped CQDs, CdS QDs will be reported in other papers. The synthesis method of N doped CQDs (Yan et al., 2015a, 2015b), no doped CQDs (Yan et al., 2016), CdTe QDs (Wu et al., 2014) and CuInS2/ZnS QDs (Liao et al., 2015) were described previously. All the QDs were dried in vacuum and stored at 0°C for further study.

The mother liquor of N,S doped CQDs, N doped CQDs, no doped CQDs, CuInS2/ZnS QDs (10,000mg/l) was prepared as follows: weigh 1000mg of QDs powder, then dissolve it in 100ml volumetric glass flask with ultra pure water, and adjust its pH to 7.0, store the solution at 0°C for study. The mother liquor of CdTe QDs (20mg/l) and CdS QDs(1500mg/l) was prepared by the method described above.

C. pyrenoidosa was inoculated in the fresh culture medium in a series cell density. The cells density (cells/ml) were determined by cell counting using the improved Neubauer Haemacytometer (Optik Labor, Lancing, United Kingdom), which corresponds to the optical density measured by UV/Vis spectrophotometer (UV-752) at the wavelength of 680nm (ODλ680). The linear equation was obtained by plotting the cell density as a function of the measured optical density.

After sufficient pre-experiments, the initial cell density of algae was set as 2.5×106 cell/ml. The C. Pyrenoidosa was exposed to a series concentrations of N,S doped CQDs (0, 1, 5, 10, 50, 100mg/l), N doped CQDs (0, 5, 10, 50, 100, 500mg/l), no doped CQDs (0, 5, 10, 50, 100, 500mg/l) and CdTe QDs (0, 0.01, 0.02, 0.1, 0.2, 1mg/l), CdS QDs (0, 0.75, 1.5, 7.5, 15, 75mg/l), CuInS2/ZnS QDs (0, 10, 20, 100, 200, 1000mg/l); three replicates were performed for each concentration. The cells density was monitored at λ680nm by UV-752. The C. pyrenoidosa growth inhibition rate was one of the indices of the QDs toxicity.

The dose-response curves of CQDs and MQDs were established following the Bűrker-Tűrk counting chamber (ISO, 1989) after a 6 days incubation period. The experiment conditions were performed the same to Section 2.3. The cells density measured at the 0h, 24h, 48h, 72h, 96h, 120h, 144h.

The toxic response is defined as growth inhibition ( I ) calculated as the relative reduction in growth rate μ caused by the test substance(Eq. (1)):
(1)
μ =
(
ln
N
n
−
ln
N
0
)
t
n
where N
0 is the nominal initial cell density, N
n is the measured final cell density and t
n is the time for the final measurement after the beginning of the test.

Percentage inhibition I for each test concentration is calculated (Eq. (2)):
(2)
I
μ i
=
(
μ
c
−
μ
i
)
/
μ
c
× 100
Where I
μi is the percentage inhibition (growth rate) for the test concentration i, μ
i is the mean growth rate for test concentration i and μ
c is the mean growth rate for the control.

For each of the identified chemicals dose-response curves were obtained by plotting I
μi against the corresponding test concentrations. EC50 is the test concentration corresponding to 50% inhibition (ISO, 1989).

The extraction process of the Chla was as follows: 10ml of the algae suspension sample was put into the plastic centrifuge tube and then centrifuged at 1300g for 15min, after which the supernatant was discarded. Then added 10ml of 95% methanol(V: V) to the centrifuge tube, put the centrifuge tube in a warm water bath (approximately 60°C) for 5min, and then centrifuged them at 1300g for 10min. Took the supernatant and monitored the absorbance at 652 and 665nm with UV-752. The Chla contents were determined using the following equation:
(3)
Chl
a
(
m g / l
)
= 18.22 ×
A
665
- 9
.55 × A
652

Protein contents were quantified according to Bradford method with BSA as standard at 595nm (Bradford, 1976). The extraction process of the protein contents was as follows: algae suspension samples (10ml) were collected at 144h in plastic centrifuge tubes, centrifuged at 1300g at 4°C for 15min, and the supernatant was removed. Then, 10ml of phosphate buffer (pH7.4, 100mM) was immediately added to each tube, and the extract was crushed by sonicator in a ice bath for 20min (power 75%, broken 2s, interval 2s). The homogenates was centrifuged at 1300g at 4°C for 15min. The determination of protein contents used Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Institute Kits.

SOD was studied as an enzymatic antioxidant. The activity of SOD was measured by NBT photochemical reduction reaction (NBT method) (Beyer and Fridovich, 1987). One unit of SOD activity (U) is defined as the amount of enzyme that yields a 50% inhibition of nitrite formation in 1ml of the reaction solution. GSH was studied as a non-enzymatic antioxidant. The GSH concentrations were determined using 5–5′-dithiobis (2-nitrobenzoic acid) colorimetric (DNTB method) (Ellman, 1959). MDA, as an in vitro marker of lipid peroxidation, was measured by thiobarbituric acid color reaction (TBA method) (Janero, 1990).
The extraction of the SOD, GSH and MDA were the same to Section 2.6. The determination of them also used Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Institute Kits.

The data were analyzed using Origin 8.0 (OriginLab, Northampton, MA, USA). Statistical analysis (One-way ANOVA) were made using IBM SPSS Statistics 19. Significant differences were accepted for p<0.05. Mean values are expressed with standard deviation (s.d.).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of six kinds QDs on algal growth
ce:section.ce:para: The growth curves of C. Pyrenoidosa under five concentrations of CQDs and MQDs were presented in Fig. 1(a, b, c and d, e, f). And the effects of CQDs and MQDs on the average growth rate of C. Pyrenoidosa were shown in Table 1.

According to the Fig. 1, during 144h (6 days), the algae density increased almost same to the control group at the low concentrations of QDs. However, at the high concentrations, QDs produced negative impacts on the algal growth and inhibited the cells growth significantly (p<0.05). And according to the Table 1, with the QDs concentration increasing the average growth rate of C. Pyrenoidosa was decreased.

ce:section.ce:section-title: EC50 values for QDs of six kinds
ce:section.ce:para: The EC50 value (mg/l) of CQDs and MQDs were determined by a growth inhibition biotest (algal cells counting) shown in Fig. 2a, b.

As shown in Fig. 2a, the EC50 value of three kinds of CQDs on C. pyrenoidosa was increased during the exposure time implied that the inhibition effects of them declined with the exposure time increasing. There are two reasons for this phenomenon: 1) the high percentages of the algae were killed but the remaining live cells could restore their growth and produced resistance, and then could recover after CQDs exposure (Kong et al., 2010); 2) CQDs are synthesized by organic matter, so the algae may have metabolic effects on them. At the 96h, the EC50 of N,S doped CQDs, N doped CQDs, no doped CQDs was 38.56, 185.83, 232.47mg/l, respectively. The toxicity order of them was: no doped CQDs<N doped CQDs<N,S doped CQDs. The results are consistent with previous studies, which have shown that different carbon nanomaterials have different toxic effects (Pereira et al., 2014; Vardharajula et al., 2012; Jiang et al., 2013). Some studies indicated that surface group of carbon nanotubes may affect the toxicity response (Sayes et al., 2006). Oppositely, as shown in Fig. 2b, with the exposure time increasing the inhibition effects of the three kinds of MQDs on C. pyrenoidosa was enhanced. The reason for this phenomenon may be that MQDs are synthesized by metals and the concentration of the released metals may be increasing with the time. At the 96h, the EC50 was 0.015, 4.88, 459.5mg/l, respectively. The toxicity order of MQDs was: CuInS2/ZnS QDs<CdS QDs<CdTe QDs.

According to experiments, the toxicity order of QDs was: CuInS2/ZnS QDs<no doped CQDs<N doped CQDs<N,S doped CQDs<CdS QDs<CdTe QDs. Previous work in our laboratory showed that CQDs at low concentrations (<50mg/l) had insignificant effects on the growth of Microcystis aeruginosa, and their toxicity were far below than MQDs (Yan et al., 2015a, 2015b). Several studies have revealed that CQDs exhibited lower biological toxicity than MQDs on plant and mammalian cells (Song et al., 2013; Feng et al., 2015). Our results are in agreement with these previous studies. Moreover, as a kind of metal-based QDs, CuInS2/ZnS QDs was observed that its toxicity was lower than carbon quantum dots. The phenomenon is meaningful because the toxicity of CuInS2/ZnS QDs have been rarely investigated and it demonstrated that not all MQDs showed higher toxicity than quantum dots contained single element.

The mechanisms of the toxicity of QDs on algae are below: 1) QDs induced the cells to produce reactive oxygen species (ROS), and the cells have oxidative damage (Finkel and Holbrook, 2000); 2) QDs release their core metals into water (Klaine et al., 2008); 3) some QDs had surface modification, and the toxicity depend on the substances doped on their surface (Hoshino et al., 2004). The toxicity of CQDs may depend on the substances doped on their surface. MQDs may release their core metals into water, and the released metal ions are toxic to aquatic organism even if at a relatively low concentration. The toxicity of CdTe QDs and CdS QDs is attributed to the core material, Cd. Stepwise stress model (SSM) indicated that a serial sequence response of organisms was activated regularly by increased toxicant concentration or exposure time (Ren et al., 2007). The detailed synthesis procedures of CdTe QDs and CdS QDs are different, and the elements introduced by the synthesis are not the same, so the toxicity of them may be significantly different. The elements of CuInS2/ZnS QDs are low toxicity materials, so its toxicity is smaller.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of QDs on Chla contents of C. Pyrenoidosa
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of CQDs and MQDs on the Chla contents at 0h, 48h, 96h, 144h are shown in Fig. 3(a, b, c and d, e, f). The effects of different concentrations of CQDs and MQDs on the Chla contents were consistent to the trend of algal growth.

The reduction of Chla is a commonly observed symptom of toxicity in algae (Romero Puertas et al., 2004). There are two reasons below: 1) The biosynthesis capacity of Chla is decreased due to the toxicity of QDs on the algae; 2) Most broken cells were observed microscopically, so the rate of Chla degradation is increased. The post-hoc was used to compare the difference within each group at 144h. Results indicate that different QDs with different concentrations compared with the control group had a significant impact on Chla contents of C. Pyrenoidosa. There was significant difference in different concentrations QDs and the higher the concentration was, the stronger the inhibitive effect was. The highest concentration of most QDs had significant difference (P<0.05) with others group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of QDs on protein contents of C. Pyrenoidosa
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of CQDs and MQDs on protein contents at 144h are shown in Fig. 4(a, b, c and d, e, f). With the concentration of QDs increasing the protein contents was decreased. The trend was consistent to the Chla contents because the protein was one of the product of photosynthesis. The post-hoc was used to compare the difference within each group. It turned out that different QDs with different concentrations compared with the control group had a significant impact on protein contents of C. Pyrenoidosa (P<0.05). There was significant difference in different concentrations QDs and the higher the concentration was, the stronger the inhibitive effect was, which consisted with the experiments of Chla contents. Generally, the higher dose groups of QDs had extremely significant difference (P<0.01) with lower dose groups, except CdTe QDs. As for CdTe QDs, it can affect the protein contents of C. Pyrenoidosa significantly, even at the low concentration (0.01mg/l). There were no there was no significant difference within each group (P>0.05) when the concentration of QDs is greater than 0.01mg/l, which demonstrated that the toxicity of CdTe QDs is extremely higher than other QDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of QDs on enzymatic activities and metabolites of C. Pyrenoidosa
ce:section.ce:para:
The effects of CQDs and MQDs on the activity of SOD, GSH level, MDA contents of C. Pyrenoidosa at 144h were shown in
Tables 2 and 3.

With the concentration of QDs increasing the activity of SOD was enhanced. These results showed that oxidative stress is increased by the QDs. A similar phenomenon was reported in C. Vulgaris (Xiao et al., 2012). With the QDs concentration increasing the GSH level was decreased. The GSH may attribute to the detoxification processes of the QDs. At high concentrations of QDs, the algae antioxidation structures may be destructed and a decrease in the level of antioxidants probably occur, which result in rapid accumulation of reactive oxygen species (ROS). Overproduction of ROS is believed to be a major mechanism of the toxicity of QDs (Sharifi et al., 2012). Lipid peroxidation is caused by ROS and enhanced when organisms are exposed to various contaminants (Valavanidis et al., 2006). It is estimated by measuring the MDA contents of cells. As a product of lipid peroxidation caused by ROS, the accumulation of MDA was enhanced when algae was exposed to QDs.

The research used C. Pyrenoidosa as a model organism to evaluate the potential environmental risks of QDs. To the best of our knowledge, it is the first time to report the toxicity evaluation of six kinds of QDs on freshwater algae at the same time and we can compare the toxicity of different QDs using the same parameters. In the experiment, we investigated the growth inhibition, acute toxicity tests, Chla contents, protein contents, the activity of enzymatic and metabolites contents of the algae. When C. Pyrenoidosa was treated by various concentrations of QDs, the Chla contents were consistent to the number of algae cells, showing a good dose-response relationship. According the EC50 of six QDs, the toxicity order of QDs was: CuInS2/ZnS QDs<no doped CQDs<N doped CQDs<N,S doped CQDs<CdS QDs<CdTe QDs. It was demonstrated that the toxicity of three kinds of CQDs were lower than MQDs except CuInS2/ZnS QDs. The toxicity of CuInS2/ZnS QDs, as far as we know, were not only the first time to compared with carbon dots but also firstly be investigated its ecotoxicity on aquatic organism. Moreover, the experiments of enzymatic activities indicated that the activity of SOD and the protein contents was enhanced and the GSH level was decreased with the increase of QDs concentration, respectively. The accumulation of MDA was enhanced when algae were exposed to QDs. In conclusion, we believe that these results are of great importance for right choice of the quantum dots in their practical applications and we convinced that the work will provide essential data for studying the downstream effects of QDs on the microalgae food chain in the future. However, further studies are needed to investigate the mechanisms deeply.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by fund of College Students Innovation Project for the R&D of Novel Drugs (no. J1030830) and the National College Students’ Innovation Entrepreneurial Training Program (G15019).
